title: A Bug's Life
facts: Released in 1998 alongside the Pixar film, A Bug's Life turns Flik's tiny world into a 3D platforming obstacle course. Traveller's Tales built the main console game, while Tiertex created the separate Game Boy Color version. Flik attacks with berries and grows useful plants from seeds, because botany is apparently the ant version of carrying a utility belt. Completing stages can unlock clips from the movie, a very late-1990s reward for surviving grasshoppers and awkward camera angles.

title: Advance Wars
facts: Colorful armies collide in this turn-based battlefield, where commanding tanks feels like orchestrating a toy-box war. Its cartoony generals bicker over territory while you plot the perfect ambush. Each map is a puzzle of terrain and tactics, forcing your brain to do more push-ups than your army recruits. Watching those tiny tanks duke it out is strangely more satisfying than real-world diplomacy.

title: Age of Empires II: The Age of Kings
facts: Age of Empires II: The Age of Kings marched onto PCs in 1999 with 13 medieval civilizations and five historical campaigns. Players gather food, wood, gold, and stone, then advance through four ages while turning peaceful villages into alarmingly busy war machines. The idle-villager button became a tiny strategic lifesaver, because one farmer staring at a tree can apparently derail an empire. Its mix of economy, formations, siege weapons, and multiplayer helped it remain active through remasters and expansions for decades.

title: Alex Kidd in Miracle World
facts: Debuting in 1986, Alex Kidd in Miracle World was Sega’s bright and bouncy take on the platforming craze. Alex can shatter boulders with his bare fists, yet Rock-Paper-Scissors duels are his real downfall. Onigiri snacks, giant fish, and punchable cars are all in a day’s work here. Mixing cartoon whimsy with random hazard chaos, it stays a weirdly charming classic decades later.

title: Among Us
facts: Among Us launched in 2018, but its great social-deduction explosion arrived two years later when streamers turned emergency meetings into appointment viewing. Crewmates complete maintenance tasks while one or more Impostors fake productivity and quietly remodel the ship with sabotage. Accusations are usually based on evidence, body language, or the highly scientific principle that red looked suspicious. It transformed the phrase "pretty sus" into an international diplomatic incident.

title: Armored Core VI: Fires of Rubicon
facts: Armored Core VI: Fires of Rubicon arrived in 2023 as the series' first new entry in roughly a decade. Players assemble highly customizable machines from interchangeable frames, weapons, generators, and boosters before taking mercenary jobs on Rubicon 3. Its stagger-focused combat rewards relentless pressure, careful positioning, and knowing when a twelve-meter robot should dodge sideways. The garage is half engineering lab and half fashion runway, except every accessory launches missiles.

title: Beyond Oasis
facts: Beyond Oasis arrived on the Genesis in 1994 and was released as The Story of Thor in Europe and Japan. Ancient developed it, with Yuzo Koshiro serving as producer and composer while his sister Ayano Koshiro handled the artwork. Prince Ali's gold armlet summons four spirits tied to water, fire, shadow, and plants. It is essentially an action-adventure where royal jewelry doubles as a supernatural customer-service desk.

title: BioShock
facts: Submerged in the art deco horror of Rapture, you’ll question if plasmids or self-control is the real superpower. Big Daddies roam the watery corridors with drill arms that could open a can of tuna in one spin. Genetic splicing grants you fire from your fingertips—convenient for toasting marshmallows, if not for moral dilemmas. It's an undersea carnival of philosophical nightmares and flying wrenches.

title: BioShock Infinite
facts: BioShock Infinite traded underwater gloom for the floating city of Columbia in 2013. Booker rides Sky-Lines like an armed commuter, while Elizabeth opens Tears that pull useful objects and inconvenient realities into battle. Barbershop arrangements of songs written decades after 1912 quietly signal that the timeline has been behaving badly. It is a patriotic carnival, quantum puzzle, and extremely awkward escort mission rolled into one.

title: Bloodborne
facts: Yharnam is a dark carnival of nightmares where top hats meet tentacles in Victorian gothic style. You hack and slash werewolves at midnight, collecting weird vials like a macabre trick-or-treat run. Every corner hides a monstrous surprise, ensuring you question your life choices with each lamp you light. It's all about stylish fear: even the bosses have better fashion sense than your average runway.

title: Borderlands
facts: Wasteland meets neon paint in this loot-shooter carnival, where guns have bigger personalities than the bandits. Claptrap’s snarky chatter is your constant companion, whether you like it or not. Every chest yields random firearms so wacky, you’ll be shotgunning skags with elemental confetti. It’s basically a road trip with psychos, giant cacti, and the promise of a bazillion guns.

title: Captain Commando
facts: Captain Commando blasted into arcades in 1991, leading a ragtag squad that includes a baby genius in a mech. The Captain himself can fry baddies with zany sci-fi gadgets, all while sporting a visor that screams ‘futuristic swagger.’ Each stage brims with over-the-top boss fights and neon-laced mayhem. It's so delightfully outrageous that you’ll swear Saturday morning cartoons came to life.

title: Castle of Illusion Starring Mickey Mouse
facts: Mickey leaps into a land where toy soldiers and living books want to ruin his day. Every stage is a whimsical fever dream, from chocolate waterfalls to haunted libraries. Mickey’s greatest weapon is his unstoppable cartoon bounce—boop an enemy, and they vanish in a puff of ‘Disney magic.’ It’s a delightfully sweet adventure that proves even illusions can’t overshadow a mouse with spunk.

title: Castlevania: Symphony of the Night
facts: Symphony of the Night sank its fangs into the world in 1997, introducing a new era of Metroidvania greatness. Alucard’s shape-shifting tricks and labyrinthine castle sparked countless speedruns and hidden-room hunts. The gothic soundtrack wraps you in a spooky blanket of organ riffs and nocturnal mystery. Even random chandeliers hide secrets, proving vampires love their cryptic home décor.

title: Chocobo's Dungeon 2
facts: Cute chocobos waddle into randomized dungeons, where cuddly meets chaotic. Monsters might look adorable, but they’ll still nibble your feathers if you wander unprepared. Magic books and potions are everywhere, making each run an enchanting lesson in unpredictable item synergy. If you think you can outrun doom on those tiny bird legs, well, you’re in for a feathery surprise.

title: Cuphead
facts: Hand-drawn mayhem meets old-school Disney vibes in a boss-rush roller coaster that’s surprisingly diabolical. Cuphead and Mugman gamble with the Devil himself, because apparently mortgages weren’t stressful enough. Each boss is a cartoon acid trip of transformations, tentacles, and flying candy. Don’t be fooled by the vintage jazz soundtrack—your reflexes will be tested like an Olympic event.

title: Dark Souls
facts: Dark Souls arrived in 2011 and sent cursed players through the interconnected kingdom of Lordran with very little hand-holding and many highly opinionated skeletons. Its combat rewards patience, stamina management, and learning exactly how many times a boss plans to flatten you. Bonfires act as checkpoints, healing stations, and cozy reminders that resting also respawns most enemies. The game's indirect storytelling, online messages, and demanding level design helped turn Soulslike into an entire genre label.

title: Destiny
facts: Space knights with laser guns roam a galaxy where dancing is somehow the universal sign of camaraderie. You’ll blast aliens, collect loot, and then dance mid-battle because what else would a Guardian do? Each raid is a puzzle-wrapped shooting gallery, starring bosses with more glowy eyes than a disco ball. It's an endless sci-fi fashion show, and you’re the model with the snazziest space cape.

title: Diablo II
facts: Demons quake in fear as you click your way through gothic dungeons for sweet, sweet loot. Potions become your best friends—and your inventory’s worst nightmare—because who needs 50 healing potions, right? Nothing compares to the moment a legendary item drops and your heart does a celebratory backflip. Hell’s minions can’t handle your unwavering obsession with shiny gear and gem-studded armor.

title: Digimon World 3
facts: Digimon World 3 hit the scene in 2002, melding RPG questing with pet-raising chaos. Exploring pixelly towns brimming with weird digital creatures is half the fun. Befriending a rookie Digimon and evolving it into a tank-sized mega-monster fuels endless battles. Between card games and side quests, it’s a virtual playground for anyone who’s ever said, ‘I just need one more Digivolution.’

title: Dino Crisis
facts: Capcom took their zombie formula, swapped in velociraptors, and unleashed prehistoric panic. Regina’s red hair is almost as fierce as the T-Rex that breaks through walls like a scaly Kool-Aid Man. Puzzles involve mixing tranquilizers or unlocking lab doors while not becoming dino lunch. It’s Jurassic tension plus Resident Evil vibes, proving dinosaurs can be even scarier than undead cannibals.

title: Donkey Kong Country 2
facts: Donkey Kong Country 2 smashed bananas in 1995, teaming Diddy and Dixie for treetop hijinks. Pirate Kremlings, roller-coaster rails, and hidden tokens make every level a monkey-flinging puzzle. Dixie’s hair-twirl jump is a showstopper, letting you dodge foes in acrobatic style. It remains a fan favorite for its funky tunes and surprisingly punishing barrel tosses.

title: Doom
facts: A lone space marine tears through demon hordes with more gusto than a chainsaw on a pinata. Pixelated gore never felt so liberating as you sprint at Mach speed across hellish corridors. Keycards, secret walls, and adrenaline-pumping metal tunes define the original corridor-blasting masterpiece. It's basically a workout routine: run, shoot, breathe, repeat—just watch out for that pinky demon.

title: Double Dragon
facts: Two brothers punch their way through post-apocalyptic streets where everyone forgot to bring civility. Jump kicks and elbow smashes solve any dispute, from street thugs to chain-wielding maniacs. Co-op means you can brawl with a buddy, although friendly fire can test that friendship real quick. It's a retro love letter to 80s hair, infinite enemies, and the unstoppable power of sibling rivalry.

title: Duck Hunt
facts: Duck Hunt first appeared for the Famicom in 1984 and became an NES launch staple in North America the following year. The NES Zapper detected targets through flashes on a CRT television, which is why the original setup does not cooperate nicely with modern flat screens. Players shoot ducks or clay pigeons while a famously smug dog retrieves successes and laughs at failures. Few games have turned missing a bird into such a personal insult.

title: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim
facts: Skyrim shouted onto the scene in 2011 with epic dragon battles and cheese wheels galore. The freedom to yeet bandits off cliffs using your Thu’um never grows old. Modders have turned this snowy kingdom into everything from Thomas the Tank Engine nightmares to Randy Savage dragons. Even if you’re on your tenth playthrough, there’s always another cave full of trolls to scream at.

title: Eternal Sonata
facts: Eternal Sonata took the stage in 2007, blending classical music with a dreamlike realm. It imagines composer Frédéric Chopin adventuring in a half-real fantasy world as he drifts off on his deathbed. Each battle is laced with bright piano riffs and color-coded combos. It’s basically a jam session in RPG form, perfect for anyone who’s ever wanted to bash monsters to a Nocturne.

title: Fantasy Life
facts: Fantasy Life launched in 2012, letting you juggle multiple in-game careers like a part-time baker turned dragon slayer. It flaunts a sunny world full of cheeky NPCs who never tire of giving you quests. Level-5 peppered the game with hidden jokes that appear if you snoop around thoroughly. Every profession unlocks unique powers, ensuring you’ll never run out of odd jobs to tackle.

title: Frogger
facts: Frogger hopped into arcades in 1981, forcing you to dodge cars, logs, and alligators to ferry frogs home. Its star amphibian has zero fear of highway traffic, which is half the comedic tension. With every near-miss truck, you’re one ribbit away from frog jelly. It remains a timeless reminder that crossing the road can be an epic gaming ordeal.

title: Galaxy Game
facts: Galaxy Game appeared at Stanford University in 1971 and ranks among the first coin-operated video games. Bill Pitts and Hugh Tuck adapted Spacewar! to a PDP-11, charging ten cents per play or three games for a quarter. Two players piloted the Needle and the Wedge around a star whose gravity was determined to ruin otherwise sensible steering. A later version could run several matches at once, impressive engineering for a machine that still had no realistic chance of paying for itself.

title: GoldenEye 007
facts: GoldenEye 007 infiltrated the Nintendo 64 in 1997, two years after the film it adapted. Rare built objective-based missions that asked Bond to do more than simply clear rooms, while four-player split-screen turned living rooms into suspiciously competitive spy agencies. The multiplayer mode was added relatively late in development and still became one of the game's defining features. It helped prove first-person shooters could thrive on consoles, even if everyone kept arguing about whether choosing Oddjob was legal.

title: Golden Sun
facts: Golden Sun shines as a 2001 RPG that emphasizes puzzle-driven dungeons and elemental powers. It features a silent protagonist named Isaac who masters Psynergy to reshape the terrain. The Djinn system allows quirky combos that boost stats or unleash colossal summons. Originally intended as one large game, it was split in half, fueling endless speculation among series fans.

title: Gotcha
facts: Gotcha debuted in 1973 as a mischievous arcade oddity from Atari. It earned a reputation for its comical “maze chase” concept and bizarre pink control domes. Some players joked it felt like grabbing two grapefruits while fleeing cartoonish enemies. Yet behind the silliness, Gotcha helped shape early competitive arcade gaming.

title: Hamurabi
facts: Hamurabi began in 1968 as Doug Dyment's text-based King of Sumeria for the FOCAL programming language. Across ten turns, the player buys land, plants grain, feeds citizens, and hopes rats or plagues do not demolish the royal spreadsheet. The familiar one-m spelling came from tight memory limits and then followed the game into later BASIC versions. It helped establish resource-management games, proving simulated citizens complained about budgets long before graphics were fashionable.

title: Hollow Knight
facts: Hollow Knight burrowed into the scene in 2017, weaving a bug-filled wonderland of hidden horrors. Each tunnel offers hand-drawn gloom punctuated by nail-biting boss fights. With every new charm or weapon, you feel more confident braving chittering minions in the deep. Its eerie soundtrack whispers encouragement as you confront the kingdom’s tragic secrets, one cryptic corridor at a time.

title: Katamari Damacy
facts: Katamari Damacy rolled onto PlayStation 2 in 2004 with a premise that sounds like a royal apology written during a fever dream. After the King of All Cosmos destroys the stars, the Prince rebuilds them by rolling an adhesive katamari that collects everything from thumbtacks to people and eventually entire landmarks. Designer Keita Takahashi emphasized novelty, simplicity, humor, and the peculiar joy of making a ball become unreasonably enormous. Its eclectic vocal soundtrack is nearly as famous as the sight of screaming pedestrians becoming celestial building material.

title: Kingdom Hearts 2
facts: Kingdom Hearts 2 landed in 2005, once again jumbling Disney magic and Final Fantasy theatrics. Sora’s spiky hair pairs surprisingly well with Donald’s quacking rage against Heartless. The flashy combat system introduced reaction commands that felt like theme-park rides made of light shows. It’s a testament to friendship, key-shaped blades, and the wild notion that Goofy can be an armored knight.

title: The Legend of Zelda: Majora's Mask
facts: Majora’s Mask emerged in 2000, pitting Link against a giant moon with an alarming grin. This time-loop mechanic forces you to relive the same three days, spawning frantic fetch quests before everything resets. Transforming with magical masks is half the thrill, from Deku-scrub flutters to Goron ground pounds. It’s a weirdly charming dance of dread, culminating in an ocarina melody that can stall an apocalypse.

title: Mario Tennis (Game Boy Color)
facts: Mario Tennis on Game Boy Color brought a tiny tennis RPG into the Mushroom Kingdom in 2000. Players level up a custom character, then challenge the big names like Mario and Bowser. It was also notable for its Link Cable support, letting friends duke it out court-side. Fans debate whether Waluigi’s first handheld appearance overshadowed every single power shot.

title: Mega Man Battle Network
facts: Mega Man Battle Network jacked into the Game Boy Advance in 2001 as a launch title in Japan. It reimagines Mega Man as MegaMan.EXE, a NetNavi operated by schoolboy Lan Hikari in a world where nearly every appliance is online, which seems wonderfully unsafe. Battles combine real-time movement on a grid with folders of collectible Battle Chips and powerful Program Advances. The result is part RPG, part deck builder, and part reminder not to connect the oven to the criminal internet.

title: Mega Man Legends
facts: Mega Man Legends soared into 3D in 1997, trading side-scrolling levels for open-world treasure hunts. MegaMan Volnutt unearths ancient ruins while tangling with a family of comic-book pirates. Its bright towns bustle with quirky NPCs, always ready to compliment your buster arm or tease your helmet. It’s proof that robots, humor, and heartfelt storytelling can happily collide in the same game.

title: Minecraft
facts: Minecraft mined its way into our hearts in 2011, building a pixel-block sandbox of infinite creation. Creepers lurk behind corners, ready to blow up your carefully designed chicken farm. Redstone contraptions can turn you into an accidental engineer of bizarre machines. It's a blocky wonderland of emergent chaos that proves even a dirt hut can feel like home.

title: Orphen: Scion Of Sorcery
facts: Orphen: Scion Of Sorcery is based on the anime 'Sorcerous Stabber Orphen' and launched in 2000. It blends platforming, puzzles, and constant witty banter from the sarcastic sorcerer himself. Some Western players were baffled by references to anime-only storylines. Despite the confusion, its oddball humor remains a highlight for fans.

title: Parasite Eve
facts: Parasite Eve emerged in 1998 as a hybrid of survival horror and JRPG flair. Aya Brea’s mitochondria-fueled powers lit up New York with flaming rats and opera-induced mutations. Between cinematic cutscenes, you micromanage DNA for edgy stat buffs, proving science can be scary. It’s a sweaty, haunting ride that taught us never to trust mitochondria in designer heels.

title: Phantasy Star
facts: Phantasy Star rocketed onto the Master System in 1987, bridging swords and sci-fi in a galaxy at war. Alis leads a ragtag crew across desert planets and bioengineered dungeons. First-person corridor crawls stunned the 8-bit crowd, who gasped at polygon-like turns. This cosmic epic carved a new path for JRPGs, proving star systems are a perfect backdrop for wizardly catpeople.

title: Pokémon Colosseum
facts: Pokémon Colosseum arrived in Japan in 2003 and traded the series' grassy routes for the dusty Orre region. Its protagonist Wes is a former Team Snagem member who steals corrupted Shadow Pokémon from criminals, a surprisingly bold career pivot for a Pokémon hero. Most battles use the double-battle format, so pairing abilities matters more than simply sending out the biggest dragon. Purifying Shadow Pokémon takes patience, friendship, and an alarming amount of walking.

title: RuneScape Classic
facts: RuneScape Classic opened its low-polygon gates in 2001, luring rookies into a world of cluelessness. You might whack goblins for hours only to discover there’s an entire bustling city next door. Everyone’s chat bubble dripped with rumors of obscure quests, or that time they got scammed for 10k gold. Despite its rudimentary look, it lit the spark for a worldwide MMO craze.

title: Shark Jaws
facts: Shark Jaws, surfacing in 1975, let players dodge an endless set of chomping teeth in pixelated waters. Rumors claim it tried to piggyback off Spielberg’s Jaws hype without the license. The game’s simplistic graphics hide a surprising intensity—one wrong move and you’re fish food. Even so, it gave arcades a playful scare long before modern horror titles.

title: Sonic 3D Blast
facts: Sonic 3D Blast was Sonic’s step into isometric platforming back in 1996. Developed by Traveller's Tales in collaboration with Sonic Team, it still features Flickies from classic Sonic lore. The angled perspective can leave players second-guessing a spring’s trajectory. Even so, it’s remembered for bridging 2D classics and Sonic’s future 3D escapades.

title: Spacewar!
facts: Spacewar! from 1962 is often hailed as one of the earliest digital games, predating mainstream arcades. Developed by inquisitive students on a hulking mainframe, it pitted two ships in a gravitational dance. Players marveled at the concept of computer-based dogfights long before the era of home consoles. Its code inspired countless spinoffs, proving that cosmic shootouts never go out of style.

title: Threads of Fate
facts: Threads of Fate debuted in Japan in 1999 as Dewprism and reached North America the following year. Its two campaigns follow Rue, an earnest amnesiac who can transform into monsters, and Mint, a banished princess whose ambition could fill several kingdoms. Square deliberately gave it a lighter tone after members of the team had worked on the much heavier Xenogears. The result is a compact action RPG where world-saving shares screen time with insults, pastries, and magical sibling drama.

title: Xenogears
facts: Xenogears launched in 1998 as a Square RPG that mixes on-foot martial arts with battles between towering machines called Gears. Tetsuya Takahashi and Kaori Tanaka first conceived its scenario as a proposal connected to Final Fantasy VII before it became its own project. Its story digs into psychology, religion, identity, and enough symbolism to keep discussion boards employed indefinitely. Production limits pushed much of the second disc toward narrated scenes, creating one of gaming's most famous cases of having more plot than calendar.

title: Zombies Ate My Neighbors
facts: Zombies Ate My Neighbors arrived in 1993 as LucasArts' colorful salute to B-movie horror. Zeke and Julie rescue oblivious neighbors from zombies, werewolves, giant babies, chainsaw maniacs, and nearly every monster that could escape a video-store shelf. Their arsenal includes water pistols, exploding soda cans, silverware, and weed whackers, because conventional weapons were apparently too sensible. The two-player mode became a cult favorite and an excellent way to discover which friend steals every health pack.
